title: Single Chicken Nesting Box
summary: A small angled-roof nesting box built from a 1x12 floor-and-side frame with cedar fence picket walls and roof — a simple standalone box rather than a full coop.
description:
This is just the nesting box itself, not a coop — a 1x12 floor-and-side frame with a 20° angled top edge on the sides, walled and roofed in cedar fence picket boards. Build the basic box with 2" screws and a countersink bit, then add the top supports before moving to the walls.

The front and back are cedar fence picket boards attached over the frame — **a scrap 1x4 works just as well for the front if that's what you have on hand**, since the plan itself substitutes one on the original build. Dry-fit the roof boards before nailing anything down, working from the bottom of the angled roof up to the top edge so each board's overlap sits correctly on the one below it.

**Raising the floor is a legitimate option** if you want the nesting box up off the ground a bit more than this build's default height — the plan notes this explicitly as a simple modification.

cutList.note: Longest point measurement, one end cut at 20° off square.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: From the 4 ft 1x12, cut two sides 16 1/2" long at the longest point, each with one end cut at 20° off square, and the floor 12" long — all at the board's full 11 1/4" width, which uses 45" of the 48". That 20° cut across an 11 1/4" side drops the low end about 4", so each side runs roughly 16 1/2" at the back and 12 1/2" at the front; that difference is the roof pitch. From the 2 ft 1x2, cut two top supports 12" — note that a 24" piece is exactly two 12" cuts with nothing left for the saw kerf, so start from a slightly longer offcut. From the two cedar fence pickets, cut four front and back boards 13 1/2" and three roof boards 15 1/2". The 12" floor between two 3/4" sides makes the box 13 1/2" wide overall, which is why the front and back boards are that length.

steps.title: Build the box
steps.body: Stand the two angled sides parallel with their 20° cuts sloping the same way, and set the 12" floor between them at the bottom so the box measures 13 1/2" across the outside. Countersink and drive three 2" exterior screws through each side into the floor's edges. Check that both sides are square to the floor and that their high ends line up with each other, because the roof lands on those two angled edges and any twist here shows up as a gap under the roof boards.

steps.title: Add the top supports
steps.body: Fit the two 12" 1x2 supports between the sides at the top, one along the high back edge and one along the low front edge, sitting flush with the angled top edges so the roof boards have something to screw into along their whole run. Fasten with 2" screws driven through the sides into the ends of each support. Lay a straightedge from one support to the other before you go on: it should touch both along its length, because that is the plane the roof follows.

steps.title: Add the front and back
steps.body: Attach the cedar picket boards across the front and back with 2" exterior screws into the sides and into the edge of the floor. Two boards at 5 1/2" give 11" of coverage per face, which does not reach the 16 1/2" back or quite fill the roughly 12 1/2" front, so decide where you want the opening left — a hen has to be able to step in over the front, and the back is better closed up. A scrap 1x4 works just as well as a picket for the front if that is what you have on hand, which is what the original build used. Cedar needs no finish, so leave the rough face out if you like the look.

steps.title: Add the roof
steps.body: Dry-fit all three roof boards before driving anything. Each is 15 1/2" long on a 13 1/2"-wide box, so it overhangs 1" at each side, which is what keeps rain off the front and back joints. The 20° slope over an 11 1/4"-deep box gives about 12" of roof run while three 5 1/2" boards give 16 1/2" of material, so they are meant to overlap: start at the low front edge and work up, laying each board over the top of the one below it like a shingle course. Once the fit looks right, screw each board into both top supports with 2" exterior screws, and let the top board run past the back edge a little so water clears the box.

images.alt: Single chicken nesting box with an angled cedar picket roof
